No, so when you're running manual bid, you're using target cost or average? Average. OK. So the thing about manual bid is, and this is something I'm really keen to cover up in the training video, is when you're bidding manually, you're overriding the auction. And you're overriding the fact that Facebook knows as options fluctuate in costs what to go in at. So this is partly why auto bid works well when you've got great audience, great conversion, et cetera. The advantage of manual bid is when you want to win the auctions, and you know that your audience is already good, your creative is already good, and conversion rate is already good. So you're overriding that in the auction. The calculation that Facebook puts in is it takes into account how much you're bidding, but then also the optimized pixel, it's already looking at your conversion rate. And then it's overlaying things like your relevant score of ads and the user experience and things like that. So then that gives it the final bid. And the final bid is not based on CPA. It's based on CPM, because the auctions are all based on being able to win that auction at that specific cost. So when I do manual bid, there's a few things I'm doing. Now, if I'm scaling and I'm spending consistently, let's say, 10k a day, then I want an average bid, because I want to let Facebook have the fluctuation between higher and low as well. Facebook don't tell you what the upper and lower bands are.

## [06:03 - 09:04]

So that's something which, let's say, for your product, AOV is 45. If you set the average at 55, sometimes it will be lower, sometimes it will be higher. But overall, you have to look at things over a three to seven day period. If you're looking at daily analysis and you're looking at average, then you're probably going to make decisions which are not helping the algorithm. So this is one thing about that. When you're using max bid, that's when I like to start to go a lot higher on the bid. So I don't want Facebook to go any higher, but that's where I'm now trying to win the auction at any cost. This is in particular good if you, for example, have low relevant scores, because Facebook will not reward you for having positive relevant scores. You just make sure that you can win the auction. So to give you an example, if I have a $30 CPA target, then I might go up to three, maybe three and a half times that value as my max CPA bid. And what Facebook does is it's only bidding higher than the next person in the auction. So it's not going for $90 CPA, but it's just trying to win you that auction at that next level up. And then what happens is you get that conversion come through. Quite often, your CPA will come in a lot lower as well. And you may or may not know this, but when you are doing your max bid, make sure your budget is at least five times your bid, because that gives Facebook enough chance of at least getting one, ideally more conversions as well. And you need to give the days to run through as well. So in your situation, you've got the 45 target, you're doing 54 as your average bid, then that gives you stability. However, there's probably chances that you're missing out on more expensive auctions. So that's where it's worth testing a max bid. Again, you can do it with $100. You can do three times your CPA, even though you're not got the budget to play with. For example, there's another one that goes on the other side of the scale, which is you take a super, super high budget, let's say $1,000 or $2,000, and then do your max bid three to three and a half percent, or you do it the other way, where you have $100 budget, and then you make your max bid, let's say $500. They're just different ways of gaming the algorithm, but the key thing is you have to get those conversions in. So if your budget is too low and you're bidding for expensive auctions, and you're not getting those conversions, then you're going to suffer. All right. ## [15:07 - 18:11]

then it's an ad set problem, something that needs to be looked at. But quite often it's odd ads that have had fluctuating performance. So then you need to cycle your ads through as well. So one of the common things is looking out for high negative, especially as you start to go up the bounds on the lookalikes, you're going further and further away from your ideal customer. And quite often you may be hitting people, that just not a good match as well. And what happens is as you go through those higher lookalikes and you start to rack up those negative feedbacks, that has a massive detrimental impact on your performance as well. So I'd be pausing those ads out and refreshing and bringing new ones in as well. Also, bear in mind that when you are increasing your percentage lookalikes, there may be a different message you need to serve them. So where the 1, 2, and 3% lookalikes converted at a specific type of message, the 4, 5, even up to 10% might need something else because they're slightly further away from your ideal prospecting customer. And so therefore the messaging has to be different as well. So one way to test that is to split those lookalikes into, let's say 1 to 4 or 1 to 5%, if you know that that's got really good performance, keep iterating messages there and then break out the 5 to 7 or 5 to 10 and try a bunch of different messages there. Quite often that comes through as well. But really it's to start with the metrics, find out exactly what has changed and then figure out why and then come up with a plan to tackle that. So one of the trainings is talking about KPIs and kind of how to break those down. So I'll cover off the KPIs also with this kind of spreadsheet and the data analysis that I use and how I break that down. All it means is that 100% of that audience or 20 or whatever it is have seen the ads. So obviously you know what growth room there is for reaching people that haven't seen the ads. But the fact is, even if they've seen a particular ad, even if they've seen it two, three times, there's two things. One, they might not have actually been in buy-in mode. So it doesn't mean that you can't hit them again. Secondly, it could just be that they didn't resonate with the message in your creative. And so therefore you keep it stirring as well. So on the balance of audience versus ads, I'd say nine out of 10 times, the problem is with the ad, not the ad set, especially when you've seen good performance. Then obviously you get to a point where you've overreached and you need to explore different audiences and things like that. But people are always changing their kind of buying mindset as well.

## [27:16 - 30:17]

the setup of your account is entirely up to you whether you want one campaign per category. Like for example, if you've got multiple category store, I wouldn't have a campaign per product because that's just going to be complete overkill. I would have one per category and I'd make that the lead category as well. And then with that, keep testing and cycling products, which are the ones that are bringing people to your store because that's the first goal. And then once they're on the store, obviously take them through the journey of viewing the product, test the cross-selling in the product page as well and see if that's actually complimenting or if it's actually just taking people away as well. Then once they do view a product, once they do get to cart and they're not purchasing, then the role of dynamic product ads is then to start looking in your catalog and start bringing other items that A, either they've looked at or B, people that have looked at, items they've looked at, also looked at as well. So Facebook's doing the job of merchandising there, which some of the big stalls do as well. So if you go on Amazon, you start scrolling down the Amazon product page, there'll be items there that people have viewed that have also viewed this product. That's essentially what dynamic product ads are trying to do as well. So when it comes to funnels, especially a multi-category, multi-product store, I wouldn't go down to the product level in terms of your campaign structure. If you've got, let's say, one to four, maybe five products, and that's all you sell, and the majority of the revenue is coming from that, then again, I'd still probably not go down to the product level because you still want to be able to flex the testing within that, maybe some product seasonal, maybe some products work better with some audiences and not others as well. So specifically on the question of kind of creating the campaign structure, I wouldn't for you go down to the kind of the end level of setting that up. So in other words, you're really letting the DPA ads doing the heavy lifting, and then I'm doing kind of a mix. I know you mentioned a lot of like carousel and video ads, so I'm assuming in those carousels and videos, I would be just putting a lot of different variant products, which kind of catches people and brings them in. That changes my setup because I normally bring them right to my landing page, right to my product page, but now I'd be bringing them to a category page, right? You can do both. So if you know that you've got one lead category, then take them to your product page, but then use cross selling just to see if there is that cross behavior happening. The category pages can work as long as you've got lots of related products and the visuals are good and it's not. So like some of the standard Shopify category pages, for example, are not the greatest use experience. So I'd probably prefer to create a landing page specifically for that category and then put some copies and brand, etc., and then put the products in there. But if your product level stuff is working, then you can continue running multiple ads to specific products and then let the DPA do the heavy lifting, as you said, on crossing those together in the retargeting as well. So when I look at the offers that come in, so end of season sales, I would start bottom up in terms of the funnel. So number one, push it to existing customers. They're more likely to be easier to sell to and then push it into your retargeting and then your warm audiences. Generally, the cold audiences are going to be harder to convert. But if the offer is good, it's definitely worth testing there as well. But in terms of order of priority, I'd go to existing customers because also I imagine you're running email campaigns as well. When you combine email with Facebook ads, for me, time after time, that works far better than doing one or the other as well. So I'd start with existing customers, start to hit them up. And what you can do is like an existing customer special offer and hit them up with an email that gives them slightly a better discount or gives them a preview and says, the sale is going to go live in two days time. We're giving it to you now and then hit them up in Facebook ads as well and say the sale starts in two days, here's special access, grab the products before other people do and then work your way through retargeting. That's basically my question. I'd run them at the same time. So again, you'd probably have overlap of people that may fall into one bid, ad set or another. It's exactly the same as doing it manually. You'd select your tiers, you duplicate the ad set for those different tiers, set your manual bid. So for example, on the 15, 10 to 15, you could set one at 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, whatever you want to kind of push the boundary up to, even 100 at first. You want to test those extreme boundaries. And then you kind of run your budgets against them and then you'd be looking at the early feedback. So making sure your CPC and CTR remains good for those bids and essentially looking at are they converting and are you getting a good CPA off the back of it as well. So I would predict, so if your target was, let's say, 10 pounds, that if you went up to a 30, 35 pounds max bid target, that would probably give you really good results. But again, there's no hard and fast rule because ultimately it comes down to competition and also how other people are bidding as well. So the worst thing to happen for manual bid would be that every single advertiser out there is using manual bid and the whole thing would just be chaos. So you have to test these boundaries out and see what works for your specific audience and also making sure that the conversion rate remains good as well. To give you an example, we had a client recently and we were aiming for a $30 CPA. We went up to, I think, $90 bid. Our cost per click went up, so from something like one pack of $1.20 to about $2, but the conversion rate also went up as well. And the kind of detail behind that was that we were reaching a better quality audience who were more likely to buy and actually converted better as well. So the net impact was positive. So there are just things to bear in mind as well. That's really helpful. And just to follow up, obviously the landscape is going to be changing week to week. So if you found like, if I found like 30, 35 pounds works well, how would I cross the best way to ensure you're still paying the most optimal level on an ongoing basis from week to week? So once you've found your max bid, then you make a duplicate of that and make that your average bid. Because then what you're doing is letting Facebook work with the boundaries. If you remain at that max bid, you may lose out on auctions as well. But then also test bringing that average bid down. Because what Facebook will do is if you continue on 30, 35 pounds average bid, gradually your CPA will creep up as well. And what you want to do is slightly just keep reducing that down with duplicates of that ad set again. So again, I'll be covering this off in a specific training video. But what I would do to find the max bid, the best max bid is duplicate those ad sets,

## [36:23 - 39:24]

test those max bids. Then the ones that are working change them to average bid. And then slowly try to bring them down and find the happy medium between volume and performance as well. Well, great. ## [39:24 - 42:25]

try not to make too drastic changes especially in the cold audience and also if retargeting is not working to make sure that I'm not making the wrong decision based on is it the cold audience that's causing retargeting to not work or is it that the ads need refreshing? And so what I would be doing is on the retargeting front keeping an eye on trend performance. So number one, the ad performance versus the ad set average. So if the ad set has completely dropped and all ads have uniformly dropped that's probably a cold audience problem. If the ads are specifically jumping up and down you see one ad better than the other then it's probably an ad performance because your retargeting pot is small it's not generally going to change massively unless you're shifting stuff in the cold audience. Also within Facebook analytics keep a close eye on average how many days go past before people convert as well. So Facebook will tell you the hours and days between conversion points. So create a funnel in Facebook analytics and I'll go through this as specific training as well and have a look at what the common behaviors are between each step of the funnel where people are getting to. So for example it might be on the first journey people get to the cart page but then they need a lot more consideration to kind of get through and convert. So that's on the retargeting front. etc. then absolutely a separate account makes probably more sense but for that would keep in the same store like because you know predominantly your audience is probably going to be the same right so you just serve. The reason why that isn't so bad is because you're not competing at the ad level you're competing at the product level so for one given person in one given auction if they see one product over another as far as the customer's concerned they're very different ads and so you want to know which one's working best and then Facebook if you use the same ad set will self-optimize if you're using separate ad sets then you'll need to make the call on where you put the budget. So keep it as inside of one single ad set of both products on an ad level let Facebook decide which one to roll out.
